name: Sharing Research Software: Citable, Discoverable, and Reproducible
description: Practical, low-barrier approaches to making research software citable, discoverable, and reusable — including CITATION.cff, licensing, metadata, and FAIR principles.
author: UC OSPO Network
learningObjectives:
After this lesson, the learner should be able to:
- Explain why research software requires citation and attribution
- Add and validate a CITATION.cff file in a repository
- Select and implement an appropriate open source license
- Enhance software visibility through documentation and metadata improvements
- Connect these practices to FAIR principles for research software
- Manage reproducible environments using lightweight tools such as pixi
abstract: This lesson introduces researchers — including graduate students, research staff, and librarians — to high-impact, low-effort practices for making research software more citable and discoverable. Topics include CITATION.cff files, open source licensing, metadata improvement, reproducible environments with pixi, and connecting these practices to FAIR principles.
audience: Researchers, Graduate Students, Research Software Engineers, Librarians
notes: Lesson is in pre-alpha/early development as of 2026-05-26.
teaches: Make research software citable, discoverable, and reproducible using FAIR practices and lightweight tooling.
